message: I ate grilled chicken and hummus at this restaurant on a Friday evening and woke up at 3 AM with severe vomiting and stomach cramps. My wife who had the same dish had identical symptoms. We kept the leftover container and the chicken smelled sour when we opened it the next morning. I went to Clemenceau Medical Center and the doctor said it was food poisoning. I spoke to the manager on Saturday and he dismissed our complaint saying many people ate that night without problems.

I bought Taanayel labneh and President butter from the dairy section and noticed the labneh tub was bloated when I got home. When I opened it the smell was rancid and the texture was watery with grey streaks. The expiry date on the label showed March 2026 but the product was clearly spoiled. I returned to the store with the receipt and the manager refused a refund saying I must have left it outside the fridge. I took photos and kept the product as evidence.

I purchased one kilogram of ground beef from this butchery on Saturday morning for a family lunch. When I started cooking the meat it released a grey foam and had a chemical smell unlike fresh beef. My children refused to eat and I threw the entire batch away. The receipt shows 85000 LBP charged but the weight on my home scale was only 780 grams. I asked the butcher about the smell and he became aggressive and told me to shop elsewhere.

I collected Augmentin 1g tablets that my doctor prescribed and when I got home I noticed the blister pack expiry date was October 2024. The pharmacist did not mention this and the box was stored on an open shelf near the window in direct sunlight. I called the pharmacy and the staff said they would exchange it but asked me to come back during rush hour. I am worried other patients received expired medication from the same batch.

I bought knefeh from this patisserie for Sunday breakfast and found a dead insect embedded in the cheese layer when I cut into it. The pastry had been sitting in an open display case with no cover and flies were landing on nearby trays. I showed the piece to the cashier who laughed and offered a replacement without apologizing properly. I paid 45000 LBP for a large tray and expected basic hygiene standards especially during summer heat.

My family visited this beach club in July and my son cut his foot on a rusted metal piece sticking out from a broken ladder near the swimming area. There was no first aid station visible and the lifeguard booth was empty for over two hours. I asked staff for antiseptic and bandages and they said they do not keep medical supplies on site. We paid 35 dollars per person for entry and expected minimum safety standards.

I went swimming at this pool in August and noticed green algae floating on the surface near the children's section. The water smelled strongly of chlorine but the chemical smell could not mask the murky green color. My daughter developed a skin rash and eye irritation within hours of leaving. I reported it to the front desk and they said the filter was broken since last week but the pool remained open to paying guests.

I ate dinner at the hotel buffet and selected the tuna salad and cold seafood platter. Within six hours I had diarrhea and fever reaching 38.5 degrees. The hotel doctor on call said two other guests reported similar symptoms that same evening. I kept my room service receipt showing the buffet charge of 120000 LBP per person. The front desk manager offered a discount on spa services instead of addressing the food safety issue.

I bought a manoushe with cheese and zaatar from a street cart near Hamra at noon and noticed the dough was prepared on a wooden board covered in old flour and grease. Two hours later I had nausea and stomach pain that lasted through the night. The vendor had raw meat stored in a plastic bag next to the dough station with no refrigeration. There was no visible health permit posted on the cart.

I bought a chicken shawarma wrap and a bottle of water from the convenience store attached to this petrol station. The wrap tasted off and the chicken inside was pink near the bone. I vomited twice on the drive home and still feel weak two days later. The store clerk said the food is delivered daily but refused to show me any delivery receipt or production date on the packaging.

We hired this catering company for a family engagement party serving 80 guests. Within 24 hours over a dozen relatives reported vomiting and diarrhea after eating the chicken kebab and rice. The food arrived lukewarm and some trays had condensation inside the foil lids suggesting improper holding temperature. The caterer denied responsibility and said guests must have eaten something else. We paid 1800 dollars for the full service package.

I purchased Gandour chocolate biscuits and a jar of imported peanut butter from this shop and the peanut butter had a layer of mold when I opened it. The shopkeeper said imported goods sometimes arrive that way and refused to take the product back. The biscuit packaging was torn and the expiry date sticker looked freshly pasted over an older date. I paid 65000 LBP total and expected products safe for my children.

I ordered delivery from this mixed food service and the grilled fish arrived cold with a strong ammonia smell. I ate a small piece before noticing and developed stomach cramps within an hour. The delivery driver said the order sat at the restaurant for 45 minutes before pickup. The app showed the order marked delivered on time but the food was clearly not fresh when it reached my door in Achrafieh.

The sushi platter I ordered had fish that tasted metallic and the rice was warm which suggests it sat out too long. My friend and I both felt nauseous within two hours and she vomited at home. We paid 95 dollars for the platter and the waiter could not tell us when the fish was delivered. I asked to speak to the chef and was told he was not available.

I found cans of Heinz baked beans on the shelf with visible dents and rust along the seams. I bought one to show my neighbor and when I opened it the contents sprayed out with a foul smell. The supermarket manager said dented cans are sold at a discount but there was no discount label on the shelf. I reported this because elderly customers shop here and may not notice the danger.

I bought fresh salmon fillets from the fish counter and the flesh was mushy and grey at the edges. The display case temperature felt warm when I placed my hand near the glass. I cooked the fish anyway and my husband had an allergic-style reaction with hives and vomiting. The fishmonger insisted the product was delivered that morning but could not show any cold chain documentation.

I was given the wrong dosage of children's cough syrup — the label said 5ml per dose but the pharmacist verbally told me 15ml. My son became drowsy and I took him to the emergency room in Zahle. The pharmacist on duty could not explain the discrepancy and said the other pharmacist had left for the day. The box clearly states maximum daily dose and we exceeded it based on her instructions.

The maamoul cookies I bought for Eid had visible mold spots inside when I broke one open. They were sold as freshly baked but the date stamp on the box underneath the price tag showed production two weeks earlier. I paid 75000 LBP for a gift box and cannot give these to guests. The baker told me the mold is normal for filled pastries and refused a refund.

I noticed a strong sewage smell near the outdoor shower area at this beach resort and saw brown water pooling near the snack bar drainage. My children swam in the sea nearby and later had ear infections diagnosed at the clinic. The resort charges 40 dollars per person and advertises clean facilities. Staff told me the drainage issue is a municipality problem and not their responsibility.

I found what looked like a dead frog floating in the deep end of this hotel pool during a weekend stay in June. The pool remained open and children were still swimming when I reported it to the lifeguard. He removed it with a net but said these things happen in outdoor pools. I checked out early and requested a refund for the pool access fee of 50000 LBP per day which was denied.

The room service club sandwich I ordered at midnight contained mayonnaise that tasted sour. I ate half before realizing and spent the night vomiting into the bathroom sink. Room service charged 280000 LBP to my bill and the hotel only offered to remove the sandwich charge after I complained three times. The duty manager said kitchen staff are short-handed and mistakes happen.

I bought roasted corn from a vendor at the weekend souk and bit into a kernel that was rotten black inside. The corn was stored in a styrofoam box with no ice on a day when temperatures reached 35 degrees. I felt stomach cramps within three hours. The vendor had no gloves and handled money then touched the corn without washing hands.

The packaged hummus and tabbouleh I bought at the petrol station convenience store had swollen plastic containers. When I opened the hummus it fizzed and smelled fermented. The cashier said these items are restocked weekly but the production date on the label was over ten days old. I drive through this station daily and worry other commuters buy the same expired products.

The wedding catering service delivered a mezze platter with labneh that tasted sharply sour and fizzy on the tongue. Several guests at our table noticed the same taste and stopped eating. The event was at a venue in Jounieh and over twenty guests reported stomach illness the next day. The caterer blamed the venue storage conditions without inspecting the remaining trays we kept refrigerated.

I bought a protein bar and vitamin gummies from this wellness shop and the gummies stuck together in one lump with white crystallization suggesting heat damage. After eating two gummies I had a headache and nausea for the rest of the day. The shop owner said the air conditioning was broken last week but they kept selling temperature-sensitive supplements anyway.

I used the shared kitchen at this community market to buy pre-made salads and noticed the tuna salad was kept at room temperature for the entire afternoon market. I ate it and developed diarrhea by evening. The vendor said ice packs ran out and the municipality inspector had not visited that day. There was no visible time stamp showing when the salads were prepared.

The kafta plate I received was raw in the center when I cut it open. I returned it to the kitchen and they brought back the same plate with only the outside seared darker. I refused to eat and the manager argued that Lebanese kafta is meant to be pink inside. My dining companion who ate hers anyway vomited in the parking lot twenty minutes after we left.

I purchased Indomie noodles and noticed the seasoning oil packet inside was rancid smelling when I opened it. The outer packaging showed no damage but the inner sachet had leaked oil that stained the noodles. I checked three more packs on the shelf and two had the same problem. The store supervisor said they receive shipments from a warehouse with no climate control.

The chicken thighs I bought smelled like ammonia the moment I removed the plastic wrap at home. The butcher had stored them in an open tray above the lamb section with no separation. I drove back immediately but the butcher said all poultry smells that way and refused to exchange. I paid 120000 LBP and threw the meat away fearing contamination.

I picked up insulin from this pharmacy and the vial had particles floating inside that should not be there. The pharmacist said I should shake it before use but the product leaflet says only clear solution should be used. I refused the vial and asked for another from a different batch. She became annoyed and said I was wasting her time during a busy afternoon.

The manoushe I bought had cheese that smelled like sour milk and the zaatar tasted bitter as if the oil had gone rancid. The baker pulled the dough from a stack that sat uncovered on a shelf behind the counter all morning. I paid 25000 LBP and threw the manoushe away after two bites. Flies were landing on the dough preparation area repeatedly.

I ordered a fruit cocktail from the beach bar and found pieces of fruit that were brown and fermented at the bottom of the cup. My throat felt irritated after drinking and I noticed the blender jug had dried residue from previous drinks. The bartender rinsed it quickly without soap when I pointed it out. We paid 18 dollars for two cocktails at this premium beach.

The pool snack bar served shawarma that had been sitting under a heat lamp for what looked like hours. The meat was dry and dark at the edges and I felt sick within two hours of eating. The lifeguard on break told me the heat lamp broke two days ago but they kept serving the same tray. I paid 55000 LBP for the meal and a soft drink.

The breakfast buffet had scrambled eggs sitting in a chafing dish with a crust forming on top for over an hour. I ate some and had stomach cramps before my business meeting. The restaurant manager said buffets are difficult to manage and offered complimentary coffee. The hotel charges 45 dollars per person for breakfast and I expected food held at safe temperatures.

The falafel balls from this street stand were cold in the center and the oil in the fryer looked black and thick. I ate two sandwiches and had severe stomach pain that night. The vendor reused oil that smelled burnt and had no thermometer for the fryer temperature. I saw him drop a falafel on the ground and put it back in the display.

I bought a pre-made chicken Caesar salad from the petrol station fridge and the lettuce was slimy and brown at the edges. The chicken pieces had a grey tinge and smelled off when I opened the container in my car. I returned within thirty minutes but the clerk said refrigerated items cannot be returned once they leave the store.

Our office ordered lunch catering and the rice was lukewarm and clumped together suggesting it sat out during transport from Dahieh to Dora. Three colleagues vomited that afternoon and we missed an important client meeting. The catering invoice was 950000 LBP for fifteen meals. The company blamed traffic delays for the temperature issue without accepting responsibility.

I bought imported sunflower oil and when I poured it the color was cloudy with sediment at the bottom of the bottle. The shop assistant said shaking fixes it but the oil smelled paint-like and nothing like normal cooking oil. I used a small amount in a pan and it released thick smoke immediately. The bottle had no importer name in Arabic as required by law.

I bought a ready-made fatteh bowl from this neighborhood point and the yogurt on top was warm and separated into watery liquid and curds. I ate it during lunch and had diarrhea within four hours. The seller kept dairy products in a cooler with melted ice that had not been refreshed since morning opening at 8 AM.

I ordered the mixed grill platter and the lamb chops were served cold in the center with blood pooling on the plate. The waiter insisted this is how Lebanese lamb is served but I have eaten here for years and never had raw meat before. I paid 180000 LBP and left most of the plate untouched. My dining partner developed food poisoning symptoms the following day.

The frozen berries I bought from the freezer section were completely thawed and refrozen into a solid icy block. When I defrosted them at home the berries were mushy and fermented smelling. The freezer unit in the store was clearly malfunctioning as other items were soft too. The customer service desk refused compensation saying frozen goods are sold as-is.

I watched the butcher cut lamb on a board that had not been wiped between customers and he used the same knife without rinsing. The lamb I received had bits of dark residue on the surface. After cooking I found the meat had an unusual bitter taste and my family felt nauseous. The shop had no hand washing station visible for staff.

The pharmacy floor behind the counter was dusty and I saw the pharmacist handle cash and then touch medication bottles without gloves or hand sanitizer. Open boxes of tablets sat on a shelf next to cleaning chemicals. I asked about storage conditions for my prescription and she dismissed the question. This is a busy pharmacy near a hospital and many vulnerable patients visit daily.

I visited this patisserie and saw the baker sneeze into his hand then continue kneading dough without washing. The display case glass was smeared with fingerprints and the tongs used for pastries sat in a dirty container of water. I did not purchase anything but reported it because families buy sweets here for celebrations. There was no hairnet or gloves on any staff member.

The changing rooms at this beach club had wet floors covered in sand and hair with no cleaning all afternoon. The shower drains were clogged and standing water reached above my ankles. Mold was visible on the ceiling corners and the soap dispensers were empty. We paid a premium entry fee and expected basic sanitary facilities during peak summer season.

The pool changing area smelled strongly of urine and the showers had black mold growing in the grout between tiles. Towels provided by the club were damp and stored in an open plastic bin without lids. I reported this to management and they said cleaning staff work only in the morning. My children refused to use the showers after swimming.

My hotel room bathroom had hair on the floor and used soap bars left from the previous guest. The housekeeping supervisor said the room was cleaned but the toilet bowl had visible stains and the towels smelled musty. I paid 250 dollars per night at this four-star property and took photos before requesting a room change. The replacement room had similar issues with dusty air vents.

The falafel vendor prepared sandwiches with bare hands after handling money and scratching his face. The food prep table had no running water nearby and used napkins were piled on the same surface as the bread. Flies circled the tahini container which had no lid. This cart operates daily on a busy street in Tripoli near the old souk entrance.

The bathroom at this petrol station had no toilet paper no soap and the floor was covered in muddy footprints and oil stains. The convenience store sells food items right next to the bathroom door which stays open. I reported it to the station manager and he said the cleaning company comes twice a week. This is unacceptable for a station on a major highway.

I visited the catering company kitchen during a tasting session and saw cockroaches near the dry storage area. The chef waved it off saying old buildings always have insects. Food containers were stored directly on the floor and raw chicken sat uncovered next to prepared salads. We were considering this caterer for our daughter's wedding and will not proceed.

The cosmetics shop had testers open without covers and the salesperson applied lipstick directly on my lips with a brush she had used on another customer without cleaning. The display shelves were dusty and expired products sat mixed with new stock. I noticed a mouse trap behind the counter which suggests a pest problem. I will not return to this shop.

The shared restroom at this community market had broken faucets and no hot water for handwashing near the food stalls. Vendors handled produce and cooked food then used the same dirty cloth to wipe their hands. I saw rats near the garbage bins behind the market entrance at closing time. The municipality should inspect this site before the weekend rush.

I could see into the open kitchen from my table and watched staff handle raw chicken then plate salads without changing gloves or washing hands. The floor was slick with grease and dirty mop water pooled near the walk-in fridge door. I mentioned it to our waiter and he shrugged saying the kitchen is always busy. This restaurant is popular with tourists and locals in Byblos.

I found a cockroach crawling on the packaged bread aisle near the bakery section. I notified a staff member who stepped on it and continued stocking shelves without sanitizing the area. The bakery counter had flour and crumbs accumulated on the floor from the previous day. Refrigerated dairy products were displayed in a unit with a broken door seal.

The cutting boards at this butchery were deeply scored with dark stains and the butcher wiped them with a dirty cloth between orders. Flies landed on the displayed meat trays repeatedly and no fly traps were visible. The floor was covered in blood and bone fragments that had not been washed down. I left without purchasing because the conditions were shocking.

Staff at this restaurant wore no hairnets and I watched a cook wipe his nose with his sleeve before plating food. The dishwashing area visible from the dining room had stacks of wet plates sitting on a dirty counter. Grease coated the exhaust hood filters and dripped onto surfaces below. I lost my appetite and left after ordering only drinks.

The deli counter staff handled cold cuts with bare hands after touching the cash register. The slicer blade had dried meat residue from what looked like the previous day. I asked for ham and cheese and noticed the cheese block had mold at the corner that the staff cut around rather than discarding. I reported this to the store manager who seemed indifferent.

The beach toilets had feces on the seat and the flush mechanism was broken in two of four stalls. Toilet paper was scattered on the wet floor and there was no attendant despite a 30 dollar entry fee. My teenage daughter refused to use the facilities and we had to leave the beach early. Management said they are understaffed on weekdays.

I noticed the pool deck had bird droppings that had not been cleaned for several days near the loungers. The filter room door was open and chemical containers were stored next to snack preparation area. Staff served drinks with unwashed hands after adjusting pool chemicals. I reported this to the health-conscious members of our swim club.

The hotel gym towels were stored in a humid closet and smelled of mildew. The sauna had mold on the wooden benches and the floor was slippery with algae-like growth. I informed the wellness desk and they provided one fresh towel but did not address the sauna conditions. Guests pay a separate 25 dollar daily resort fee for these facilities.

The juice vendor squeezed oranges with hands covered in dirt from handling coins. Ice cubes were stored in an open bucket on the ground next to the cart wheel. Used cups were rinsed in a bucket of cloudy water and reused. I paid 15000 LBP for a fresh orange juice that I could not drink after watching the preparation.

The attached car wash bathroom had sewage backing up from the drain and the smell reached the convenience store area. Food items including sandwiches and pastries are sold within three meters of this bathroom. The attendant said the plumber was called but the store remained open for two days in these conditions.

During a site visit for our corporate event I saw catering staff stack dirty plates on the same cart as freshly prepared appetizers without separating them. The kitchen extraction fan was coated in grease and the floor was slippery. We cancelled our contract but they still charged a 200 dollar tasting fee claiming we gave late notice.

The supplement shop had open protein powder containers with scoops sitting in the powder without covers. A customer coughed near the display and staff did not sanitize anything afterward. Dust covered the vitamin bottles on lower shelves near the entrance where street pollution enters. I worry about contamination of products sold for health purposes.

establishment_name: Local Trade Point Sin el Fil
message: The outdoor seating area at this food point had pigeons eating from plates left on tables that were not bussed for over an hour. Staff used the same rag to wipe tables and clean food prep surfaces. Garbage bags torn open near the cooking area attracted flies throughout the lunch service. This is a popular lunch spot for office workers in Sin el Fil.

The patisserie display had flies landing on uncovered baklava trays for the entire time I waited in line. Staff used bare hands to rearrange pastries and no one wore a hairnet despite working directly over open food. The floor behind the counter was sticky with spilled syrup that had not been mopped. I walked out without buying despite needing sweets for a family visit.

I saw mice droppings near the bread storage area at the back of this bakery when I used the customer restroom that shared a wall with the kitchen. The baker denied any pest problem and said the droppings were chocolate crumbs. The health certificate displayed near the entrance appears faded and I could not read the expiry date.

The pharmacy had a strong chemical smell and expired promotional products were displayed at the front counter mixed with current stock. The pharmacist ate a sandwich at the dispensing counter and wiped his hands on his coat before handling my medication bag. I asked for a receipt and the counter surface had visible stains from previous spills.

The shelf price for Kiri cheese showed 28000 LBP but the cashier charged me 35000 LBP at the register. When I showed her the shelf tag she called a supervisor who removed the tag and said it was an old price. I had a photo on my phone taken five minutes earlier showing the lower price. They refused to honor the shelf price and argued in front of other customers.

I was charged 15 dollars for a sunbed at this beach but the sign at the entrance listed 10 dollars. The attendant said weekend rates apply but there was no mention of weekend pricing on the board. I paid because my family was already seated but I want the Ministry to investigate these hidden surcharges. Other families near us were charged different amounts for the same service.

The restaurant bill included a 15 percent service charge that was not mentioned on the menu or by our waiter. The total came to 220000 LBP instead of the 190000 LBP I calculated from the menu prices. When I questioned it the manager said all restaurants in the area add service charge automatically. I have eaten at many places and this was never disclosed before ordering.

I filled my tank with 40 liters and the pump display showed 380000 LBP but my card was charged 420000 LBP. The attendant said the pump price was outdated and the new price is posted on a small paper near the cashier window that I could not see from the pump. I asked for a printed receipt and the amounts did not match the pump meter reading.

The hotel added a city tax of 50 dollars per night that was not shown when I booked online or at check-in. The front desk said it is mandatory but the sign explaining it was behind the counter not visible to guests. My three-night stay cost 150 dollars more than the confirmed reservation price. I travel to Beirut monthly for work and have never encountered this charge at comparable hotels.

I ordered a medium pizza for delivery and was charged for a large on the receipt. The delivery app showed 18 dollars but the driver demanded 25 dollars in cash saying prices increased. I showed him the app confirmation and he became aggressive insisting I pay the higher amount. I paid under pressure but reported this immediately to protect other customers.

The electronics shop advertised a phone charger for 12 dollars on a window poster but inside they said the sale ended yesterday. The poster had no end date and was still displayed prominently. They charged me 22 dollars and the cashier said the poster is for reference only. I asked for a manager and he refused to honor the advertised price.

I bought halloumi and the scale at the deli showed 0.45 kg but the receipt listed 0.62 kg charging me an extra 45000 LBP. The staff member said the scale is approximate and the register weight is correct. I asked to reweigh and she refused saying the product was already wrapped. I kept the receipt and the cheese which I weighed at home on my own kitchen scale.

The butcher charged me for two kilograms of lamb but my home scale shows only 1.6 kilograms including packaging. I returned within an hour with the meat and receipt and the butcher said his scale is certified and mine is not. He refused any adjustment and became loud with other customers watching. I paid 340000 LBP and feel cheated on weight.

The pharmacist charged 180000 LBP for a medication I later found at another pharmacy for 95000 LBP. When I asked about the price difference she said their import channel is different. The receipt she gave me had no itemized price breakdown just a total. I believe tourists and elderly patients are being overcharged without transparent pricing.

I bought a box of maamoul and the price tag said 40000 LBP but I was charged 55000 LBP at the counter. The cashier blamed a system update and said all pastry prices increased that morning. The shelf tags had not been changed and three other customers in line had similar complaints. The manager offered no apology and processed payments at the higher rate.

The pool charged my children as adults despite them being 10 and 12 years old. The price board lists child admission at 20 dollars but I was charged 35 dollars each for all four of us. The cashier said their policy changed but the sign outside was not updated. I paid 60 dollars extra and have photos of the outdated sign at the entrance.

The street vendor charged 30000 LBP for a manoushe that is normally 15000 LBP in the area. When I asked for a price list he said he charges what he wants. He had no receipt book and refused to give change correctly handing me 10000 LBP less than owed. This happened near the corniche where many tourists walk.

I purchased motor oil and the pump attendant added a car wash fee of 15000 LBP I did not request. The receipt bundled the charges together without itemization. When I disputed it he said all customers pay the wash fee during rainy season. I filled up at this station regularly and this fee was never mentioned before.

The restaurant charged my card in dollars at an exchange rate of 89500 LBP per dollar when the official rate posted at the bank next door was 89750. The difference on a 200 dollar bill was small but the practice affects many customers daily. The manager said they set their own rate and refused to adjust. Banque du Liban guidelines on pricing transparency should apply here.

I ordered catering for forty people at 15 dollars per head but the final invoice showed 22 dollars per head with added setup and transport fees never discussed. The contract I signed listed extras as optional but they were added automatically. The company owner stopped responding to my calls after I paid a deposit of 300 dollars. I want the Ministry to review their billing practices.

The mixed service point charged 50000 LBP for a product labeled 35000 LBP on the shelf. The clerk said the label was wrong and blamed the supplier. I visit this shop weekly and the same product was 35000 LBP last week with the same label. I feel this is deliberate overcharging of neighborhood residents who do not always check receipts.

I bought a vitamin supplement and the receipt showed a loyalty discount was not applied despite my card being scanned. The cashier re-scanned twice and the system still charged full price of 85000 LBP. The manager said the promotion ended but the shelf banner still advertised buy-one-get-one. I took a photo of the banner date which showed valid through end of month.

The hotel minibar price list showed water at 3 dollars but I was charged 8 dollars per bottle on checkout. The front desk said the room list is outdated and minibar prices are set by the vendor. Three bottles were charged totaling 24 dollars instead of 9 dollars. I disputed the charge and they removed one bottle but not the full overcharge.

I was double charged on my card for a beach cabana rental of 80 dollars. The attendant swiped my card twice saying the first attempt failed but my bank shows two charges. The beach management asked me to resolve it with my bank and took no responsibility. I have bank statements showing both transactions on the same date and time.

The pharmacy gave me a receipt totaling 240000 LBP but when I counted the items at home the sum should be 195000 LBP. I returned the next day and the pharmacist said the receipt includes a dispensing fee not listed separately. She could not show me any published fee schedule. I believe the extra charge was invented at the register.

I paid 20 dollars for pool entry and was charged an additional 10 dollars for a locker that the sign said is complimentary. The receptionist said the sign is old and lockers now cost extra. No updated pricing was visible anywhere in the lobby. I needed the locker for valuables and had no choice but to pay the surprise fee.

The butchery receipt listed premium cut prices for standard lamb chops. I compared with the price board on the wall and each kilogram was overcharged by approximately 60000 LBP. The owner said the board prices are wholesale only. There was no indication that displayed prices do not apply to retail customers walking in.

I bought a birthday cake and was charged for decorative writing that the baker said is included in the catalog price. The final bill was 180000 LBP versus the 130000 LBP shown in the display book. The manager added fees for fondant and delivery to the counter that I picked up myself. I have the catalog photo showing all-inclusive pricing.

The restaurant has no health permit displayed anywhere despite operating for over a year on this street. I asked the owner and he said he applied but the municipality is slow. Other restaurants nearby display current permits prominently. I am concerned because they serve raw meat dishes and operate a busy open kitchen visible from Hamra sidewalk.

Imported canned tomatoes on the shelf had no Arabic labeling and no importer name as required. I asked the store manager and he said the shipment arrived through a new supplier. The English-only label shows a country of origin but nothing in Arabic for Lebanese consumers. The Ministry of Economy should verify these products are legally imported.

This butchery sells imported frozen meat with no veterinary certificate posted and no importer details on the packaging. I asked the butcher where the meat comes from and he could not provide documentation. The Ministry of Agriculture requires traceability for imported meat products. I did not purchase but reported this because families shop here daily.

I was sold antibiotics without a prescription when I described my symptoms to the pharmacy assistant. The licensed pharmacist was not on duty and a trainee handled the sale. The Order of Pharmacists requires a pharmacist present for controlled medications. I am reporting this because misuse of antibiotics is a serious public health concern in Lebanon.

The bakery sells packaged mouneh products with homemade labels and no Ministry of Public Health registration number. I asked about the production facility and the owner said they are made at home. Commercial sale of unregistered food products violates food safety regulations. These jars of pickled vegetables sit on shelves next to licensed commercial brands.

This beach club operates with no visible trade license or insurance certificate at the entrance. I asked management about liability coverage after seeing unsafe jetty conditions and they could not produce documents. The club hosts events with hundreds of guests on weekends. Municipal approval for commercial beach operation should be displayed publicly.

The water park has no safety inspection certificate posted near the main attractions. I asked the manager about the annual inspection required for public pools and he said it is pending. The park opened this season without any visible permit from the Ministry of Public Health. Families with young children deserve confirmation that rides and pools are inspected.

The hotel restaurant buffet operates without displaying a current food handling permit in the dining area. I asked the hostess and she directed me to a framed document that expired in 2024. The hotel is a well-known property hosting international guests and should maintain current certifications. Fire safety certificate near the elevator was also outdated.

The street food cart near the university has no municipality vendor permit and no health certificate posted. It operates daily from 7 AM to midnight serving hundreds of students. I asked the vendor for his permit number and he said he does not need one for a small cart. The municipality has rules for all food vendors regardless of size.

The convenience store at this petrol station sells packaged food and tobacco but has no separate trade license displayed for the shop. Only the fuel station license is on the wall. Food items are stored in conditions that require a food retail permit. I asked the cashier and she said the station license covers everything.

This catering company delivered food to our office with no health certificate copy as required in the contract. I requested documentation before our company event and they sent a blurry photo of an expired 2023 certificate. Our HR department requires valid certification for all food vendors. The company operates from a residential building in Dekwaneh.

The shop sells imported cosmetics with no Arabic ingredient labels and no Ministry of Economy approval stickers. I checked three skincare products and none had the required importer information. The shopkeeper said these are parallel imports and legal. Consumers have a right to know product contents in Arabic as mandated by Lebanese consumer protection law.

This neighborhood point sells homemade jams and pickled goods without production facility registration or labeling compliance. The jars have handwritten dates and no nutrition information. I asked the seller about her license and she said small producers are exempt. The Ministry of Economy has specific rules even for small-scale commercial sellers at markets.

The restaurant expanded outdoor seating onto the sidewalk without a municipality permit for public space usage. They placed barriers blocking pedestrian walkway on a narrow street in Achrafieh. I asked to see the permit and the manager said it is being processed. This has been ongoing for three months with no visible authorization posted.

I found baby formula on the shelf with Arabic labels that appear photocopied and pasted over the original packaging. The importer name is missing and the batch number does not match the box bottom code. I reported this to the store manager who removed one box but left others on display. Infant formula requires strict import documentation.

The pharmacy was operating after 10 PM with no licensed pharmacist on duty as confirmed when I asked for the pharmacist registration number. A cosmetic sales assistant dispensed my prescription medication and said the pharmacist would sign later. The Order of Pharmacists hotline confirms a pharmacist must be present during all operating hours.

Imported olive oil bottles at this shop have no Arabic label and no Ministry of Economy import approval number on the packaging. The shop owner said the shipment came through a port clearance without full documentation. These products compete with legally imported brands on the same shelf at lower prices. Consumers cannot verify authenticity or safety.

The beach snack bar sells alcohol without a visible liquor license displayed as required by law. I asked the bartender and he said the license belongs to the main resort office. No copy is posted at the point of sale where alcohol is served to hundreds of guests daily during summer. This was reported to resort management without response.

The waiter brought the wrong dish twice and when I asked for the correct order he sighed loudly and slammed the plate on the table. We waited over 45 minutes between courses on a quiet Tuesday evening with only four tables occupied. I asked to speak to the manager and the waiter said he is the manager. We left without dessert and he made no effort to apologize.

I stood at the deli counter for twenty minutes while three staff members chatted behind the counter ignoring customers. I finally left without buying the items I needed for dinner. The supervisor was standing nearby on her phone and did not intervene. This supermarket is the only large store in our neighborhood in Jal el Dib and the service is consistently poor.

The butcher refused to trim the fat from my lamb order despite this being standard service at every other butchery I use. When I insisted he threw the meat on the scale aggressively and told me to go elsewhere. Other customers heard the exchange and one left as well. I have been a customer for five years and this treatment was humiliating.

The pharmacist was dismissive when I asked questions about side effects of my new medication. She said she does not have time to explain and pointed to the leaflet. When I asked about interactions with my blood pressure pills she said Google it. I am a 68-year-old patient who relies on professional guidance and this attitude is unacceptable.

I ordered a custom cake for my son's birthday with his name written in blue icing. The cake arrived with the wrong name and green icing. The baker said she was busy and could not redo it on the day of the party. We paid 160000 LBP in advance and had to buy a replacement cake from another shop on short notice.

The beach club refused to refund my entry fee when it rained and all water activities were suspended for three hours. The manager said rain is not their responsibility despite us arriving specifically for swimming. We paid 120 dollars for a family of four and sat under a leaking umbrella. Other families were also denied refunds that afternoon.

The pool receptionist refused to accept Lebanese pounds and demanded payment in US dollars only. I offered LBP at the daily market rate and she said cash dollars only is their policy. No sign at the entrance states this currency restriction. I had to leave and find an ATM because my children were already changed into swimwear.

The hotel front desk lost my reservation confirmation and kept me waiting forty minutes at 1 AM after an international flight. They blamed my booking website and assigned me a smoking room when I had booked non-smoking. The night manager was rude and said take it or find another hotel. I was exhausted and had prepaid 400 dollars for three nights.

The street vendor refused to give me a receipt when I asked for one after buying nuts for 45000 LBP. He said street vendors do not give receipts and became angry when I insisted. I wanted the receipt for my expense report. He raised his voice and other pedestrians stopped to watch the argument.

The petrol station attendant was on his phone for the entire time I waited for him to activate the pump. I honked politely and he gestured rudely for me to wait. When he finally came he filled the wrong fuel grade and blamed me for not specifying. I had to wait another twenty minutes for the tank to be drained.

The catering company arrived two hours late for our corporate lunch leaving eighty employees waiting. No one called to inform us of the delay and the sales representative stopped answering her phone. When food arrived it was disorganized and the setup team was rude to our office manager. We will not use them again and want others warned.

The electronics shop refused to honor their return policy when I brought back a faulty charger within three days with receipt. The salesman said opened items cannot be returned despite the policy posted on the wall saying seven-day returns. The store manager was not available and staff told me to come back next week. I paid 35 dollars for a product that never worked.

The staff at this neighborhood point ignored me for fifteen minutes while attending to friends who arrived after me. When I finally got attention the clerk was on a personal phone call and served me without ending the call. I asked a simple question about product availability and she shrugged without answering. This happens regularly and I am tired of the disrespect.

Our waiter forgot our order entirely and when we reminded him after thirty minutes he blamed the kitchen. The manager offered nothing except saying it is a busy Friday night. We had theater tickets and missed the first act waiting for food that arrived cold. The restaurant was only half full and the poor service was inexcusable.

The hotel concierge promised to book a taxi for 6 AM and it never arrived. I missed my flight and the concierge denied making the arrangement despite my written note with his signature. The duty manager offered 50 dollars hotel credit which does not cover my rebooking fee of 200 dollars. I have stayed at this hotel many times and expected reliable service.

The beach attendant assigned us a sunbed in the shade when we paid for front-row beds as shown on our reservation email. He said first come first served overrides reservations. We showed the confirmation on my phone and he walked away without resolving the issue. We paid premium pricing for location that was not honored.

The pharmacy cashier refused to call the pharmacist for a consultation saying he is on lunch break for another hour. I needed advice on whether to take medication before or after food and the cashier said she is not allowed to advise. I waited forty minutes and the pharmacist still had not returned when I left. A healthcare facility should have coverage during lunch.

The delivery driver from this catering service threw the bags on my doorstep and drove away without checking the order. Half our items were missing and sauces spilled over the containers. I called the restaurant and they said delivery is outsourced and not their problem. We hosted guests and had to improvise the meal. Unacceptable for a 250 dollar order.

The clothing store saleswoman followed me suspiciously through every aisle and made a comment about my appearance when I browsed sale items. I felt discriminated against and left without purchasing. I have shopped at luxury stores across Beirut and never been treated this way. I asked for the store manager and she said she is the owner and does not welcome complaints.

The pool manager refused entry to my nephew because he has a visible birthmark that the staff incorrectly assumed was a contagious skin condition. This was humiliating in front of other guests at the entrance. I explained it is a birthmark he has had since birth and offered a doctor note. They still refused and did not apologize for the embarrassment caused.

I called this restaurant to reserve a table for eight for a graduation dinner and they confirmed by phone. When we arrived they had no record and seated us at two separate small tables in the back near the kitchen. The hostess said phone reservations are not guaranteed. We celebrated a milestone in terrible conditions because of their disorganization.

The supermarket security guard followed my elderly mother and accused her of putting an item in her purse at self-checkout. He searched her bag publicly and found nothing. The item was still on the shelf where she had looked at it. No apology was given and the manager said the guard was doing his job. She was shaken and refuses to return to this store.

I had dinner at this rooftop restaurant in Gemmayze on a humid July night and ordered the raw meat kebiyeh. Within four hours I was vomiting repeatedly and my temperature reached 39 degrees. The meat tasted overly warm when served though the plate looked chilled with parsley on top. I kept the receipt for 195000 LBP and photographed the leftover portion. The manager said their supplier is trusted and refused to report the incident internally.

I bought two tubs of Taanayel yogurt and a pack of Kiri cheese from the refrigerated aisle at this supermarket in Dora. Both yogurt lids popped open easily and the contents smelled sharply acidic. The cheese had a slimy film though the printed date suggested two more weeks of shelf life. I returned with receipt 44218 and the duty manager blamed a power outage from the day before without removing affected stock.

I purchased lamb shanks from this butcher in Tripoli old souk area for Eid lunch. The meat surface felt tacky and left a brown residue on my cutting board before cooking. While simmering the stew released a sour odor that filled the kitchen. My mother and I both had diarrhea that evening. The butcher insisted the meat was slaughtered that morning but showed no slaughterhouse stamp on the wrapping paper.

I collected a box of Panadol Extra and a bottle of cough syrup for my daughter at this pharmacy near Saifi. At home I noticed the syrup had separated into dark sediment and clear liquid layers. The pharmacist had stored it on a shelf above a heater vent. When I called back the assistant said shaking fixes it and refused exchange because I had left the premises.

I bought a tray of baklava and a box of maamoul from this patisserie in Achrafieh before visiting family in the mountains. The baklava filling tasted fermented and the phyllo was soggy as if stored in humidity. Two relatives who ate two pieces each felt nausea during the drive to Faraya. I paid 90000 LBP and the shop clerk said summer heat affects texture but offered no refund.

My friends and I spent the afternoon at this beach club near Jiyeh in August. I ordered a chicken tabbouleh wrap from the beach bar and noticed the chicken pieces were lukewarm with a grey tint near the edges. I ate half before stopping and spent the night with stomach cramps and chills. The bartender said the kitchen is far from the beach and food cools during delivery.

I took my children to this water park in Jounieh during school break. We bought chicken nuggets and fries from the poolside kiosk and my son vomited in the car on the way home. The fries were soft and the nuggets had a sour interior when I broke one open to check. I paid 48000 LBP for the meal and the kiosk worker said the fryer oil was changed recently without evidence.

During a two-night stay at this mountain hotel in Mzaar I ate the dinner buffet lamb stew. I woke at 2 AM with severe abdominal pain and visited the clinic in Kfardebian the next morning. The doctor noted signs of bacterial food contamination. The hotel food and beverage manager offered a late checkout but would not acknowledge that other guests reported similar symptoms at breakfast.

I bought a chicken shawarma sandwich from a cart on Bliss Street near AUB around 11 PM. The rotisserie meat looked dried and was shaved from the outer dark layer only. Two hours later I had violent diarrhea and dehydration. The vendor had no refrigeration for toppings and the garlic sauce container sat open in the heat. There was no permit posted on the cart frame.

I grabbed a packaged tuna sandwich and a chocolate milk from the fridge at this petrol station on the Damour highway. The sandwich bread was soggy and the tuna smelled ammonia-like when I opened it at the office. I threw it away after one bite and felt nausea all afternoon. The cashier said deliveries come twice weekly but could not show the delivery log.

We booked this catering firm for a baptism lunch in Zahle serving sixty guests. The fattoush and grilled chicken trays arrived two hours late and were barely warm. By evening at least eight guests messaged our family group about vomiting and fever. We paid 1400 dollars including service and the owner blamed our venue oven for not reheating properly.

I purchased a jar of imported olives and a tin of sardines from this specialty shop in Hamra. The sardine tin was bulging and sprayed oil when I pierced it with the opener. The olives had white mold floating in the brine under the lid. The shop owner said imports sometimes swell in transit and offered a ten percent discount on my next visit instead of a refund.

I bought a pre-packed chicken shawarma plate from this small food counter in Bourj Hammoud market hall. The garlic sauce tasted fizzy and the chicken had pink centers though it was labeled ready to eat. I felt stomach pain within an hour at work in Dora. The seller said the microwave at home would finish cooking it though it was sold as prepared food.

I ordered the fish sayadieh at this seafood restaurant in Tyre harbor area on a Friday. The rice beneath the fish was warm but the fillet center remained translucent and jelly-like. My husband and I both felt ill before midnight with matching symptoms. We paid 240000 LBP and the waiter argued that fresh catch is always softer than frozen fish.

I picked up a family pack of labneh balls and hummus from the deli section of this mini market in Sin el Fil. The labneh exterior was yellowing and the hummus had a sharp alcoholic smell suggesting fermentation. My children refused to eat and I noticed the fridge thermometer display was blank. The owner said the device broke last month but products remain safe.

I bought veal cutlets from this butcher in Baabda for a dinner party. The vacuum seal had air pockets and the meat color was purplish rather than red. After pan searing the cutlets released greenish liquid. One guest left early with stomach upset. The butcher claimed the color comes from young calf meat and refused to inspect the remaining cuts I brought back.

I was dispensed eye drops at this pharmacy in Jal el Dib and only at home read the leaflet showing storage below 25 degrees. The bottle felt warm and had been kept in a window display facing afternoon sun. The drops caused burning worse than my original irritation. The pharmacist on duty the next day said many products tolerate brief warmth.

I purchased manoushe with labneh and zaatar from this bakery in Mar Mikhael on my commute. The labneh was runny and tasted carbonated on the tongue. The zaatar mix smelled musty as if the sesame seeds were old. I discarded it after two bites and still felt mild nausea. The baker said summer humidity affects labneh and continued selling from the same batch.

At this beach resort in Chekka I ordered a fruit platter and noticed several melon pieces had fermented edges turning transparent. My daughter ate a few pieces and developed stomach pain and mild fever by evening. We paid 22 dollars for the platter plus service. The pool attendant who took the order said the kitchen prepares fruit in the morning for the whole day.

I used the hotel pool cafe at this resort in Summerland and ordered a club sandwich. The bacon smelled off and the mayonnaise tasted sour though the bread was toasted. I reported feeling dizzy and nauseous to the lifeguard who called a supervisor. The supervisor offered a complimentary juice but did not remove the sandwich from the menu that day.

The room service burger at this airport hotel arrived with pink juices pooling in the box and a sour smell from the patty. I ate half before realizing and spent the night vomiting. The charge was 320000 LBP plus a delivery fee. The night manager said the kitchen was understaffed and offered to remove only the delivery fee from my bill.

I bought roasted chestnuts from a seasonal cart near Souk el Tayeb in Beirut. Several nuts inside the bag were moldy black when I cracked them open. The vendor stored cooked nuts in a closed plastic bag in direct sun for hours. I felt a burning stomach sensation after eating three good ones mixed with the bad batch. He refused to return my 20000 LBP.

I purchased a ready-made pasta salad from the convenience store at this Medco station in Dekwaneh. The salad container lid was not sealed and pasta pieces were slimy with a vinegar-like spoilage smell. I ate a few bites at my desk and had diarrhea before the workday ended. The attendant said the supplier replaces items every three days without date labels.

Our company ordered breakfast boxes from this catering service for a training session in Beirut Digital District. The cheese manakish in several boxes had sour cheese and the fruit cups leaked fermented juice. Four trainees reported illness by afternoon. Facilities paid 28 dollars per person and the caterer claimed our air conditioning caused condensation inside the boxes.

I bought protein powder from this supplement store in Verdun and noticed the inner seal was broken and powder clumped into hard balls. After one shake I had bloating and stomach cramps lasting two days. The salesperson said clumping is normal and refused exchange because I opened the tub. The product had no Arabic ingredient label on the imported container.

I got a chicken liver sandwich from a food stall inside the Bourj Hammoud parking market. The liver tasted metallic and undercooked with soft pink centers. I vomited twice that night and missed work the next day. The stall operator had no gloves and reused the same knife for raw and cooked items. I paid 35000 LBP and kept the foil wrapper as evidence.

I watched the cook at this shawarma shop in Sidon handle raw chicken then slice finished shawarma without washing hands or changing gloves. The prep table had dried sauce and lettuce leaves from the previous day stuck to the surface. Flies circled the meat rotisserie during the lunch rush. I reported it to the owner and he told me to eat elsewhere if I am bothered.

The fish section at this supermarket in Tripoli had melting ice under displayed sea bream and water pooling around boxes of shrimp. The seafood attendant used the same gloves to handle cash and fish fillets. A strong ammonia smell filled the aisle. I spoke to the floor manager and he said the ice supplier was late and they cannot discard fresh catch.

I entered this butcher in Zahle and noticed flies landing directly on hanging lamb carcasses near the entrance. The sawdust floor was soaked with blood that had not been replaced in days. The butcher wiped his knife on an apron stained black before cutting my order. I left without buying and photographed the conditions through the open shop front.

At this pharmacy in Hamra I saw the technician eating a manoushe at the prescription counter then handle my medication bag without washing hands. Open bottles of cough syrup had dust on the caps and a dead cockroach lay near the baseboard behind the waiting chairs. I asked for the pharmacist and was told he works only mornings though the store stays open until midnight.

The open knefeh tray at this sweet shop in Tripoli had no cover and pigeons walked on the windowsill inches from the display. Staff used bare hands to portion knefeh and the syrup tray had dead insects floating on top. I refused to buy and told the owner who said birds are everywhere in the old city and cannot be controlled.

The public showers at this beach in Batroun had mold covering the lower walls and a drain clogged with hair and sand. The changing room benches were wet and sticky and no cleaner was visible during peak Saturday hours. We paid 25 dollars entry per person and my wife refused to use the facilities. Management said they hire cleaners only on weekdays.

The indoor pool at this sports club in Antelias had cloudy water and band aids floating near the ladder steps. The shower area smelled of sewage and the floor was slippery with algae. Lifeguards sat in chairs looking at phones without scanning the pool. I reported it to the membership desk and they said chemical levels were fine despite visible debris.

My hotel room at this property in Byblos had stained bedsheets with hair from a previous guest and a bathroom sink coated in toothpaste residue. Housekeeping knocked once and left when I stepped out for ten minutes without finishing the room. I paid 180 dollars per night during festival weekend and took photos before asking for a supervisor who offered only one night credit.

The falafel stand near the corniche in Tyre prepared food on a wooden board blackened with old oil. The same cloth wiped the counter and the vendor face throughout the afternoon. A bucket of wastewater sat next to uncooked falafel mix with no lid. Tourists lined up unaware of the conditions behind the counter.

The restroom at this IPT station in Baabda had fecal matter on the toilet seat and no cleaning log visible on the wall. The convenience store sells sandwiches directly beside the bathroom door which stays open to a hallway with no ventilation. I reported it to the station supervisor and he said the cleaning contractor comes every other day.

I toured the kitchen of this catering company in Dekwaneh before signing a wedding contract and saw rat droppings near flour sacks. Staff stored cleaned trays on the floor next to muddy shoes. The tasting samples were prepared on unwashed boards used for raw chicken minutes earlier. We walked out and they still billed a 150 dollar tasting deposit.

The cosmetics counter at this shop in ABC Ashrafieh had testers with no covers and brushes caked in dried product from previous customers. The salesperson dipped her finger into a cream jar to show me texture after handling cash. Dust coated the shelves and a broken ceiling tile exposed insulation above the product display.

The food court area in this community market in Nabatieh had overflowing garbage bins and cats jumping on tables where vendors prepare manoushe. One vendor wiped plates with a rag that had fallen on the concrete floor. I reported it to the market administrator and he said weekend crowds make cleanliness difficult.

I dined at this Lebanese restaurant in Jbeil and saw cockroaches crawl along the baseboard near the salad bar. When I notified the waiter he stepped on one and continued serving salads without cleaning the area. The dining room trash bin had no lid and smelled of rotting food. I paid 165000 LBP and left most of my meal untouched.

The bakery section inside this supermarket in Jal el Dib had flour dust coating every surface and no hairnets on staff shaping dough. A worker sneezed into his elbow then continued handling bread rolls. The display case cooling fan was covered in grease dust. I notified customer service and they said the bakery is operated by a tenant not the supermarket.

This poultry shop in Bourj Hammoud stored raw chicken in plastic bags on top of an unrefrigerated counter during a power cut. When power returned the bags remained unrefrigerated for another hour while staff served customers. The floor was slick with feather debris and wash water. I asked about cold chain safety and the owner waved me away.

The shelf tag for Al Wadi tahini showed 42000 LBP but the register charged 58000 LBP for the same jar size. The cashier said the supplier raised prices overnight and tags update when staff have time. I photographed the shelf label at 4 PM and the charge happened at 4 PM the same day. The supervisor removed the tag while I was still at the counter.

This beach club in Amchit charged 40 dollars for a cabana on the phone but demanded 55 dollars at arrival claiming premium weekend pricing. The booking confirmation email showed no mention of surcharges. My group of six paid extra under pressure because we had already driven from Beirut with elderly parents. Other guests were quoted different prices at the gate.

Our bill at this grill restaurant in Zahle included items we never ordered including two extra arak glasses and a salad. The total was 310000 LBP instead of the 230000 LBP we calculated. The owner said the waiter added a mandatory mezze platter for tables over four people without telling us. The menu has no such policy printed anywhere.

I filled 30 liters at this Total station in Jounieh and the pump showed 285000 LBP but my card receipt was 312000 LBP. The attendant said the pump screen lags behind the central system price. I asked for a printed receipt matching the pump and he delayed ten minutes then produced a different total. This happened on two consecutive visits.

This boutique hotel in Hamra added a resort fee of 35 dollars per night at checkout not mentioned during booking or check-in. The receptionist pointed to a laminated note behind the desk that guests cannot see from the counter. My four-night stay included 140 dollars in surprise fees. I travel for work monthly and this practice feels deliberately hidden.

The delivery app showed 14 dollars for my order but the driver demanded 22 dollars in cash claiming the restaurant raised prices. The receipt inside the bag listed yet a third amount in LBP equivalent. I paid under pressure because my children were waiting for food. The restaurant phone line was busy for thirty minutes after.

I bought a phone case advertised at 8 dollars in the window of this electronics shop in Dora. Inside they said the display model price expired and charged 18 dollars. The window poster had no end date and was updated only after I argued. The salesman said posters are suggestions not binding offers.

The neighborhood grocer in Chiyah charged me 75000 LBP for sunflower oil marked 52000 LBP on the shelf. The owner said the label belongs to an old shipment while new stock costs more. He had both prices mixed on the same shelf without distinction. I bought one bottle under protest and kept the receipt and shelf photo.

I bought halloumi at this butcher deli counter and the digital scale faced away from me during weighing. The receipt showed 0.8 kg but my kitchen scale measured 0.55 kg after removing wrap. The clerk said their scale is legally certified and mine is not. I was overcharged roughly 70000 LBP based on my measurement.

This pharmacy charged 220000 LBP for a generic blood pressure medication I found listed at 145000 LBP at two other pharmacies on the same street in Tripoli. The receipt listed only a total with no itemized price. When I asked why the pharmacist said their importer charges premium for fast delivery.

I ordered a custom birthday cake priced at 110000 LBP in the display book at this patisserie. At pickup they demanded 175000 LBP citing gold leaf decoration I never requested. The written order form shows no decoration options selected. The manager said fondant work is always extra despite the catalog showing one all-inclusive price.

The water park charged adult prices for my 11-year-old despite the entrance board listing child rates up to age 12. The cashier said their system classifies anyone over 140 cm as adult. My son is tall for his age but the sign makes no mention of height rules. We paid 45 dollars extra for two children.

A juice vendor at the Sidon souk entrance charged 25000 LBP for pomegranate juice while a sign behind him said 15000 LBP. He said the sign is old and pomegranate season prices increased. He had no receipt book and shortchanged me 5000 LBP on change from 50000 LBP. This happened in front of two other customers charged different amounts.

This Shell station attendant charged a car wash fee of 20000 LBP without asking though I declined when prompted at the pump. The receipt bundled fuel and wash together. When I refused the wash he said the system already charged and I must speak to the manager who was not on site.

The restaurant added a currency conversion markup of three percent on my dollar payment without disclosure on the menu or bill footer. I paid 150 dollars for a group dinner and the receipt showed an unexplained LBP equivalent higher than the central bank daily rate. The manager said all Hamra restaurants apply the same markup.

We hired this caterer for a memorial lunch quoting 12 dollars per plate for fifty people. The final invoice included kitchen rental and transport fees totaling 980 dollars instead of the agreed 600 dollars. The contract attachment listing extras was not shared until after the event. The owner stopped answering messages once we paid the deposit.

This furniture showroom in Metn advertised a sofa set at 1200 dollars on social media but in store demanded 1650 dollars citing fabric upgrade. The post showed the exact fabric I selected in the showroom. The salesman said online prices exclude delivery and assembly though the post said free delivery.

establishment_name: Local Trade Point Sin el Fil
message: The mixed convenience shop in Khalde applied a ten percent card surcharge I was not told about until after swiping. A small note behind the register says surcharge applies but faces the wall not the customer. My grocery total was 180000 LBP plus 18000 LBP surprise fee. The clerk said cash avoids the fee but I had no cash.

This sushi restaurant in Beirut Marina operates with no food handling permit visible and staff could not produce one when asked. A framed certificate near the restroom is faded beyond reading. Neighboring restaurants display current Ministry of Public Health permits at the entrance. They serve raw fish daily to tourists without posted certification.

I found imported baby cereal on shelves with English-only labels and no Arabic nutrition facts or importer contact. The store manager said the shipment cleared customs and needs no Arabic sticker. Lebanese law requires Arabic labeling for consumer goods. I photographed three brands with identical missing information.

This butcher sells imported Brazilian beef with stickers showing no slaughterhouse approval number and no Arabic translation. I asked for the veterinary import certificate and the owner said it is at the warehouse. He could not show any document during my visit. The meat is displayed prominently as premium Angus.

I purchased allergy medication without prescription after describing symptoms to a cosmetic salesgirl at this pharmacy in Dora. The licensed pharmacist was not present and the security guard said he covers pharmacy breaks. The Order of Pharmacists requires a pharmacist on duty for all dispensing. This endangers patients seeking proper counseling.

This bakery sells jars of homemade mouneh pickles with handwritten labels and no registration number from the Ministry of Public Health. The owner said home producers do not need permits for small quantities. She sells dozens of jars daily from a commercial storefront on a main road in Baabda. Competitors display valid registration codes.

This private beach near Jbeil hosts weddings without displaying an entertainment license or occupancy limit certificate at the entrance. Security could not show any municipal approval when overcrowding blocked fire exits during an event I attended. Guests paid 50 dollars per ticket for an event exceeding visible seating capacity.

The kids splash zone at this aqua park reopened this season with no updated safety inspection sticker on the main slide structure. Staff said inspection is scheduled next month but rides operate daily. Parents receive no disclosure at the ticket booth. A cracked step near the slide ladder is taped but not repaired.

This airport area hotel operates a rooftop bar serving alcohol past midnight with no liquor license copy posted as required. I asked the bartender for the license number for an event inquiry and he said only management has it. The bar hosts loud parties visible from nearby residential buildings.

The kaak seller at weekend market in Souk el Ahad has no vendor permit displayed and told me he has never applied because police do not check. He sells hundreds of kaak with sesame and cheese fillings from an open cart. Food is stacked uncovered while he collects cash with the same gloves.

The mini market inside this petrol station sells tobacco and vaping products with no separate retail license visible. Only the fuel station permit hangs on the wall. The cashier said tobacco sales are included in the fuel license which I doubt. Imported vape pods have no Arabic warning labels.

We requested the catering company health certificate before a school event and received an expired 2022 document. The operations manager said renewal is processing but could not show a pending application. Our school policy requires current certification for any vendor feeding children.

This toy shop sells plastic baby bottles imported from an unknown supplier with no Ministry of Economy conformity sticker. The packaging is entirely in a foreign language with no importer address in Lebanon. The shopkeeper said stickers are optional for small shipments.

The corner shop in Ain el Remmaneh sells imported energy drinks with Arabic labels that look photocopied and peel off in my hand. The barcode searches to a product not registered in Lebanon. The owner said goods arrive through informal channels at lower prices.

The waiter at this restaurant in Monot ignored our table for twenty-five minutes after seating us though the dining room was half empty. When we flagged him he said we should have waved harder. We asked for water three times before it arrived warm without glasses cleaned properly. The manager never appeared despite two requests.

I waited forty minutes at the butcher counter while one employee served his friend chatting in the back. Other customers left before being served. When I finally got attention the butcher rushed my order and cut the wrong thickness despite clear instructions. He refused to redo it saying he is too busy.

The pharmacy technician spoke to me sarcastically when I asked whether to take antibiotics before or after food. She said I should read the box myself and turned away to chat with a friend visiting behind the counter. I am recovering from surgery and needed clear guidance. Other patients in line heard the exchange.

I ordered a wedding cake tasting at this patisserie and the baker forgot our appointment leaving us waiting thirty minutes in a hot storefront. When she arrived she had no samples prepared and offered yesterday leftovers from a display cake. We had driven from Tripoli for the appointment and she showed no apology.

The beach club hostess lost our prepaid reservation and seated us far from the water despite paying for front row sunbeds. She argued that email confirmations are not binding and first arrival gets priority. We showed the payment receipt on my phone and she shrugged without calling a supervisor.

The pool reception refused entry because my membership card was demagnetized though I had the app showing active status. The clerk would not call the head office and demanded I purchase a day pass for 30 dollars. I had visited weekly for two years and the lack of assistance was insulting.

The hotel concierge promised to arrange a taxi for 5 AM and none arrived. I missed an early flight and the morning staff claimed no note was left by night shift. The hotel manager offered breakfast vouchers worth 20 dollars after I spent over 300 dollars on a new ticket.

The corn vendor near Raouche rocks became angry when I asked for a receipt after paying 20000 LBP. He raised his voice saying street food has no receipts and waved his tongs at me. Families with children stepped back. I was simply documenting a work expense.

The petrol station manager accused me of scratching his pump when I questioned the meter reading. He spoke loudly in front of other customers implying I was trying to cheat the station. Security footage later showed I never touched the pump housing. I felt humiliated over a 15000 LBP dispute.

The catering driver dumped trays on my doorstep without setup as contracted and left before I could inspect the order. Missing vegetarian meals left six guests with nothing to eat at a formal dinner. The company owner said drivers are not waiters and refused partial refund.

The clothing store clerk refused to let me exchange a shirt with tags attached within their posted seven-day policy. She said sale items are final though the receipt does not state that exception. The manager hid in the back office and staff told me to return next week when he might be available.

The clerk at this service point in Hadath hung up when I called to ask about a refund for a defective charger. In store he told me phone complaints are not recorded and I must accept store credit only. I paid 45000 LBP cash and needed a refund not credit.

I ate the chicken tawook platter at this restaurant in Furn el Chebbak and noticed the meat was pink and cold near the bone. My colleague who ordered the same dish had stomach cramps before we returned to the office. We paid 145000 LBP each and the cashier said the kitchen was closing and rushed the order. I kept the takeaway box and the meat smelled sour within an hour at room temperature.

I bought a family pack of Gandour mini croissants and a carton of Taanayel chocolate milk from this grocery in Verdun. The croissants had green mold spots inside the packaging visible through the clear window. The milk tasted burnt and separated into chunks when poured. The store manager removed only the croissants and said the milk was fine because the date was valid.

I purchased minced beef from this butcher in Aley for kafta and the package leaked reddish fluid in my car trunk. At home the meat had an overpowering sulfur smell. I discarded all 1.2 kg and still felt nausea from the smell exposure. The butcher charged 155000 LBP and refused any compensation saying vacuum packs always leak.

I was given a generic antibiotic instead of the brand my doctor specified at this pharmacy in Nabatieh. The pills looked different from my previous prescription and caused intense stomach burning within hours. The pharmacist said generics are identical but the leaflet listed different inactive ingredients. I stopped taking them and returned for the correct brand.

The sfouf cake I bought from this bakery in Tripoli for a condolence visit had a bitter chemical aftertaste and a dense wet center. Two family members spat it out at the gathering. I paid 65000 LBP and the baker said turmeric quality varies by season. I believe old baking powder or spoiled oil was used.

I ordered a shrimp cocktail at this beach restaurant in Damour and the shrimp tails were black and mushy. I ate two before stopping and developed vomiting and fever by midnight. The waiter said shrimp darken when grilled though these were served cold in cocktail sauce. We paid 38 dollars and the manager offered no medical assistance.

My son ate a hot dog from the snack bar at this pool complex in Faraya and found the sausage gray inside when he bit it. He vomited at the lodge within an hour. The vendor reheated sausages in a microwave without covering and the fridge door hung open in the heat. I reported it to the park office and they said the vendor is independent.

The hotel breakfast omelette station used eggs that smelled sulfurous when cracked on the griddle. I noticed shells with dried residue suggesting they were not fresh. After eating I had mild food poisoning symptoms and the nurse on call said two other guests complained. The buffet costs 38 dollars and no ingredient sourcing information was available.

I bought a mixed nut cone from a vendor outside Beirut Souks and crunched into a rancid almond that tasted like paint. Several nuts in the batch smelled stale when I checked at home. The vendor stored open nuts in plastic bags exposed to car exhaust on the street. I paid 30000 LBP and he refused to replace the cone.

I purchased a chicken mayo sandwich from the fridge at this Coral station in Antelias and the bread edges were green with mold under the wrapper fold. The attendant said sandwiches are delivered daily but the label showed a production date three days earlier. I threw it away without eating and want the station inspected.
